title: 1.0 Rule of Cool
content:
> *Sometimes the best story is the one that breaks the rules.*

## **Core Rule**
The **Rule of Cool** allows a character to attempt actions that are **normally not permitted** by the established system rules when the outcome would **enhance the narrative, tension, or excitement** of the scene.

This rule exists to support **cinematic moments**, **player creativity**, and **memorable storytelling**, without undermining the integrity of the Asteria system.

---

## **GM Authority**
- All uses of the Rule of Cool are **GM-approved**
- The **GM controls the outcome**, resolution method, and consequences
- The GM may:
  - Require one or more rolls
  - Increase difficulty or DCs
  - Impose resource costs or damage
  - Apply partial success or failure
  - Introduce narrative complications

> *The Rule of Cool grants permission to attempt — not permission to succeed.*

---

## **When the Rule of Cool May Be Used**
The Rule of Cool may be invoked when **one or more** of the following are true:

- The action is **cinematic, dramatic, or stylish**
- The action enhances **player engagement or table excitement**
- The moment is **high-pressure or climactic**
- The idea is **creative but mechanically restricted**
- The outcome would create a **memorable story moment**

---

## **When the Rule of Cool Should NOT Be Used**
The Rule of Cool should not be used when:

- It trivializes a major threat or encounter
- It permanently bypasses progression systems
- It invalidates another character’s specialization
- It removes meaningful risk or consequence
- It becomes a repeatable exploit or expectation

---

## **Resolution Guidelines**
When resolving a Rule of Cool attempt, the GM should apply **one or more** of the following:

- Elevated difficulty or disadvantage
- High-risk or contested rolls
- Exhaustion, wounds, or resource loss
- Temporary or situational effects only
- Narrative fallout or unintended consequences

---

## **One-Time Exception Clause**
Any benefit granted through the Rule of Cool is considered a **one-time narrative exception** unless explicitly stated otherwise by the GM.

> *The Rule of Cool bends the rules — it does not rewrite them.*

---

## **Player Expectations**
- Players may **propose**, but never demand, Rule of Cool actions
- Creativity is encouraged; entitlement is not
- Failure under the Rule of Cool should remain **interesting and meaningful**

---

## **GM Safety Valve**
The GM may **modify, restrict, delay, or deny** the use of the Rule of Cool at any time if:

- The action threatens campaign tone or internal logic
- The action undermines system balance
- The action removes tension or meaningful choice
- The action overshadows another player
- The action risks becoming precedent or exploitation

The GM is **not required to justify or explain** the decision.

> *The Safety Valve exists to protect the story, the system, and the table.*

---

## **Design Intent**
The Rule of Cool exists to ensure that:
- Narrative momentum is never strangled by mechanics
- Player creativity is rewarded, not punished
- High-pressure moments feel cinematic and alive
- The system remains flexible without becoming fragile
---
> *Asteria is built on rules — but legends are forged when those rules are bent, not broken.*

title: 1.1 Combat Turn Time Rule
content:
> *Combat should feel dangerous, urgent, and alive — not like a planning meeting.*

## **Core Rule**
Combat in Asteria is designed to **flow quickly and decisively**, maintaining tension and momentum.

- **In-game combat round:** 6 seconds
- **At-table turn time:** Limited to maintain pacing and urgency

This rule exists to prevent excessive deliberation and ensure combat remains **engaging for all players**.

---

## **Standard Turn Time**
- Each player has **1 minute** to declare and begin their action on their turn.
- The timer begins when the GM announces the player’s turn.

> *You do not need to finish resolving the action within the time — only commit to it.*

---

## **What Counts as “Declaring an Action”**
A player is considered to have acted once they clearly state:
- Their **primary action**
- Any **movement** taken
- Any **target** involved

Dice rolling and resolution may continue after the declaration.

---

## **Allowed During Other Turns**
Players are encouraged to:
- Plan actions ahead of time
- Ask clarifying questions outside their turn
- Measure ranges or check **Talents** and **Skills** before their initiative

---

## **If a Player Exceeds the Time Limit**
If a player has not declared an action when the time expires, the GM may choose one of the following:

- Allow a brief **10–15 second grace period**
- Reduce the action to a **basic or defensive action**
- Delay the turn until the end of the initiative order
- Default the character to a **safe, instinctive action** (e.g. Dodge, Guard)

> *The goal is momentum, not punishment.*

---

## **Time Extensions clause**
The GM may extend a player’s turn time when:

- The player is new to the system
- A new mechanic or Talent has just been unlocked
- The situation is tactically complex
- A Rule of Cool moment is being proposed
- A Heroic Stand moment is being played out

### Recommended Extensions
- **Standard extension:** +30 seconds
- **Complex or cinematic moment:** +1 minute
- **Climactic or boss encounter:** Turn limits may be temporarily relaxed

---

## **High-Pressure & Climactic Combat**
During:
- Boss fights
- Narrative finales
- Life-or-death moments

The GM may:
- Relax turn timers
- Allow table discussion
- Prioritise narrative clarity over strict pacing

---

## **GM Authority**
The GM has final authority to:
- Enforce, extend, or suspend turn timers
- Adjust pacing to suit the table
- Override this rule to preserve enjoyment and fairness

---

## **Player Expectations**
- Be ready when your turn begins
- Know your core abilities
- Keep combat moving for the whole table

Players are **never punished** for asking reasonable questions — but repeated delays may be addressed.

---

## **Design Intent**
This rule exists to:
- Maintain combat tension
- Respect table time
- Keep all players engaged
- Prevent analysis paralysis
---
> *Hesitation in battle has consequences — act with intent, or react under pressure.*

title: 1.2 Heroic Stand
content:
> *When death is certain, legend is a choice.*

## **Core Rule**
In the **final moments of a character’s life**, a player may choose to perform a **Heroic Stand**.

A Heroic Stand represents a character pushing beyond mortal limits for one final, defining action — buying time, changing fate, or leaving a mark on the world.

This rule is **narrative-first** and exists to create **legendary final moments**, not optimal play.

---
## **Trigger Conditions**
A Heroic Stand may be declared when **all** of the following are true:

- The character would otherwise die or be rendered permanently incapacitated
- Escape or survival is no longer reasonably possible
- The player **chooses** to stand rather than fall
- The GM confirms the moment is narratively appropriate

> *Heroic Stand is always voluntary. It is never forced.*

---

## **Emphasis Roll**
A Heroic Stand always succeeds in **happening**.  
The roll determines **how powerful and impactful** the stand is.

- No AC checks are required
- Attacks **automatically hit**
- The Emphasis Roll determines:
  - Number of actions
  - Damage output
  - Area of effect
  - Narrative consequence

---

## **Heroic Stand – Emphasis Roll Outcomes**

### **Critical Success**
*(Natural max or GM-defined critical)*

- The character performs at **absolute peak potential**
- Maximum possible output for a single turn
- Examples:
  - A martial character unleashes up to **4 attacks**
  - A spellcaster casts up to **4 spells** or one overwhelming spell
- Damage may be **maximized**
- The moment becomes **legendary**
- May permanently alter the battlefield or story

---

### **High Success**
- The character performs **above mortal limits**
- Multiple attacks or effects
- Significant damage or impact
- The stand meaningfully changes the encounter

---

### **Partial Success**
- The character lands **one decisive action**
- Damage or effect is solid but limited
- The stand buys time, saves allies, or weakens the enemy

---

### **Low Success**
- The character manages a **single meaningful strike or act**
- Damage is reduced or partially effective
- The action still matters narratively

---

### **Failure**
- The character does not achieve the intended effect
- However:
  - The stand still occurs
  - The moment is cinematic
  - The failure creates story impact (delay, distraction, sacrifice)

> *A failed Heroic Stand still becomes part of the legend.*

---

## **Action Scope**
During a Heroic Stand:
- Normal action economy is **suspended**
- The GM determines reasonable limits
- Abilities, spells, and attacks may be chained narratively

This is not a rules exploit — it is a **story climax**.

---

## **Aftermath**
Once a Heroic Stand is resolved:
- The character **dies**
- Resurrection is:
  - Impossible (As the Tobias - The God Of Death grants you this final moment

The consequences of the stand **echo forward**.

---
## **GM Authority**
The GM has full authority to:
- Approve or deny a Heroic Stand
- Determine roll thresholds
- Shape narrative consequences
- Limit excessive mechanical abuse

The GM may **modify outcomes** to protect tone, pacing, and balance.

---
## **Player Expectations**
- Heroic Stand is not a tactic — it is a farewell
- Players are encouraged to:
  - Speak final words
  - Describe their actions
  - Embrace the moment fully

---
## **Design Intent**
Heroic Stand exists to:
- Turn character death into story
- Reward sacrifice over optimization
- Create unforgettable table moments
- Ensure deaths feel earned, not abrupt

---
> *Not all heroes survive.  
But some are remembered forever.*

title: 1.3 Rolling With Emphasis
content:
> *Sometimes success and failure are not enough — what matters is how far you push.*

---
## **Core Rule**
**Rolling With Emphasis** is a narrative resolution mechanic used when the **quality of success or failure matters more than binary outcomes**.

Instead of determining *if* an action succeeds, this rule determines **how well or how poorly** it succeeds.

This system is intended for:
- High-pressure moments
- Dramatic uncertainty
- Situations where failure should still move the story forward

---
-## **How Rolling With Emphasis Works**
When Rolling With Emphasis:

- The player rolls **two d20**
- The result **furthest from 10** is used
  - Lower rolls indicate worse outcomes
  - Higher rolls indicate stronger outcomes
- If both results are **equidistant from 10**, reroll

> *This creates a wider emotional swing — stronger highs and harsher lows.*

---

## **When Rolling With Emphasis May Be Used**
The GM may call for an Emphasis Roll when:

- Outcome quality matters more than success/failure
- A standard DC would feel too restrictive
- The situation is tense, cinematic, or pivotal
- Binary outcomes would harm narrative flow
- A moment deserves weight without total loss

Examples:
- Desperate last stands
- Risky gambits
- Narrative-heavy skill use
- Climactic or emotional scenes

---

## **Advantage & Disadvantage**
Rolling With Emphasis is only used when:
- The character has **neither advantage nor disadvantage**

If advantage or disadvantage would normally apply:
- The GM should resolve the situation **without Emphasis**, or
- Narratively cancel both and proceed with Emphasis

---

## **Resolution Philosophy**
Emphasis Rolls do **not** negate consequences.

Instead:
- Low rolls introduce complications
- Mid-range rolls succeed with cost
- High rolls exceed expectations
- Critical results reshape the scene

Failure should never halt the story — it should **redirect it**.

---

## **Suggested Outcome Bands**

### **1–5 | Severe Failure**
- Action fails catastrophically
- Major complication or cost
- Situation worsens dramatically

---

### **6–10 | Failure with Impact**
- Action partially fails
- Consequences introduced
- Progress is made at a cost

---

### **11–15 | Success with Cost**
- Action succeeds
- Minor complication or drawback
- Momentum continues

---

### **16–19 | Strong Success**
- Action succeeds cleanly
- Additional benefit or advantage
- Situation improves significantly

---

### **20 | Exceptional Success**
- Action exceeds expectations
- No cost, strong benefit
- Narrative momentum shifts decisively

---
## **Binary Outcome Replacement**
Rolling With Emphasis may replace rolls where the system would normally:
- Use a flat d20
- Rely on a strict DC
- Produce a pass/fail outcome

This includes:
- Skill checks
- Saving throws
- Narrative combat moments
- Environmental challenges

---
## **GM Authority**
The GM has full authority to:
- Decide when Emphasis applies
- Adjust outcome bands
- Introduce narrative consequences
- Override results to protect tone and pacing

The GM may **decline Emphasis** if it undermines clarity or fairness.

---
## **Player Expectations**
- Rolling With Emphasis emphasizes storytelling over optimization
- Players should describe intent before rolling
- Results should be embraced narratively, even when unfavorable

---
## **Design Intent**
Rolling With Emphasis exists to:
- Replace flat outcomes with dramatic gradients
- Encourage risk-taking
- Maintain narrative momentum
- Support cinematic storytelling

---
> *In Asteria, fate does not ask “did you succeed?”  
It asks “how far did you go?”*

title: 1.4 Dice by Context
content:
>The die used for a skill check is determined by **context**, not by the skill name.

## Combat & Combat-Related Actions → d20

An action uses a **d20** if it:

- Occurs **during initiative**
- Directly affects **HP**
- Directly affects **Stamina or Mana while in combat**
- Changes **positioning** (movement, reach, zone control)
- Alters **combat advantage** (advantage, disadvantage, flanking, suppression)
- Is resolved in **seconds** rather than minutes

These rolls are intended to be:
- Fast
- Swingy
- Decisive
- Tactically impactful

## Non-Combat Actions → d100
An action uses a **d100** if it:
- Occurs **outside combat**
- Resolves over **minutes, hours, days, or longer**
- Affects **systems, preparation, resources, or knowledge**
- Benefits from **degrees of success**
- Produces **quality-based outcomes**

These rolls are intended to be:
- Granular
- Progressive
- Narrative-rich

## Context Overrides Skill Name
The same skill may use different dice depending on how it is used.
**Examples:**
- **Stealth**
  - Sneaking through a city → d100  
  - Hiding or disengaging in combat → d20
- **Deception**
  - Running a long con → d100  
  - Feinting or misleading in combat → d20
- **Lockpicking**
  - Opening a lock safely → d100  
  - Opening a lock under attack → d20

## Design Intent
- **d20** resolves immediate danger and tactical decision-making  
- **d100** resolves mastery, preparation, and long-form actions  
- **Stamina and Mana** function as tactical resources only when combat pressure is present  
- Skill progression remains unified across the system

title: AC Calculation System
content:
## 🧮 AC Formula
Final AC is calculated using the following components:

- **NAC**  
+ **Metal Base AC**  
+ **Armour Piece Modifiers**  
+ **Enchantment Modifiers**  
+ **Spell Modifiers**
- **Armour Type (Set Bonus)**  
---

## 🔧 Component Breakdown

### **NAC (Natural Armour Class)**
A base armour value influenced by race.

---
### **Metal Base AC**
Each metal type has a base AC value.

Example from your notes:  
- **High Steel Base AC = 3**

Different metals will have different base AC values.

---
### **Armour Piece Modifiers**
Each worn armour piece contributes a **percentile modifier**, which turns into AC points.

Example from your notes (High Steel Light Set):

- Cuirass — 100% → +3 AC  
- Bracers — 30% → +1 AC  
- Greaves — 30% → +1 AC  
- Pauldron — 40% → +2 AC  

**Total piece modifier = 7 AC**

---
### **Enchantment Modifier**
Magic applied to armour pieces.

Example:
- +1 Defence enchantment

This stacks on top of metal + piece values.

---

### **Spell Modifier**
Temporary magical effects that increase AC.

Example from notes:  
- Spell gives **+1 NAC**

---
### **Armour Type — Set Bonuses**
Wearing a full matching of the same type of set grants additional AC Depending on the type of armour (cloth, leather, light, medium, heavy), you gain a **maximum AC and a bonus**.

Example from your notes:  
**+3 Set Bonus**

---
## ✔ Example Calculation (From Left Note)

**High Steel Light Armour Set**

- High Steel Base AC = **3**
- Cuirass (100%) = **+3**
- Bracers (30%) = **+1**
- Greaves (30%) = **+1**
- Pauldron (40%) = **+2**

Subtotal = **7**

Set Bonus = **+3**

**Final AC = 10**

title: Asteria — Characteristics System
content:
## 🧬 Overview

Characteristics represent a character’s core physical, mental, and spiritual attributes.

They are:

- Rolled at character creation
- Determined by Size Category
- Used in all major systems (combat, skills, magic, resources)

---

🎲 Characteristic List

Characteristic| Type| Function
Strength| Primary| Physical power, melee damage
Dexterity| Primary| Precision, accuracy
Agility| Primary| Speed, reflex, Initiative
Constitution| Primary| Health scaling (HP)
Endurance| Secondary| Stamina scaling (SP)
Intelligence| Primary| Knowledge, spell complexity
Wisdom| Primary| Mana scaling (MP), awareness
Charisma| Primary| Social influence
Luck| Special| Fate-based mechanic

---

🎲 Characteristic Rolling System (Locked)

🔹 Core Rule

All Characteristics are rolled individually using dice determined by the character’s Size Category.

- Each stat is rolled separately
- Results must be taken as rolled
- No swapping, assigning, or rerolling

---

🧬 Size-Based Dice Table

Size| STR / END / CON| DEX / AGI| INT / WIS / CHA| LCK| Player Legal
Miniature| 1d4| 4d6| 4d6| 3d6| ❌ GM Only
Tiny| 2d4| 4d6| 4d6| 3d6| ✅
Small| 3d6| 4d6| 4d6| 3d6| ✅
Medium| 4d6| 4d6| 4d6| 3d6| ✅
Large| 4d8| 3d6| 4d6| 3d6| ✅
X-Large| 4d8| 2d6| 3d6| 3d6| ⚠ GM Only
Huge+| —| —| —| —| ❌

---

🎯 Rolling Process

1. Choose Size Category
2. Roll each stat using the table
3. Record results
4. Stats are locked after rolling

---

🔢 Tier System (Locked)

Characteristics scale using a Tier System.

- Every 20 points = 1 Tier
- Tiers provide flat bonuses

Tier| Value Range| Bonus
I| 1–19| +0
II| 20–39| +1
III| 40–59| +2
IV| 60–79| +3
V| 80–100+| +4

«Tier bonuses apply to:

- The characteristic
- All related skills»

---

🔗 Primary vs Secondary Characteristics

🔹 Primary

- Always apply Tier bonuses
- Used in rolls, skills, and systems

🔹 Secondary (Endurance)

- Does not apply bonuses automatically
- Must be unlocked via:
  - Profession
  - Talent
  - Special ability

---

❤️ Resource System (Locked Integration)

Resources are calculated using:

HP = Race Roll + Constitution + Modifiers  
SP = Race Roll + Endurance + Modifiers  
MP = Race Roll + Wisdom + Modifiers

🔹 Components

Race Roll

- Determined at character creation

Characteristic

- Full value is used (no multipliers)

Modifiers

- Items
- Spells
- Effects
- Conditions

---

⚡ Derived Effects

System| Characteristic
Initiative| Agility
HP| Constitution
SP| Endurance (if unlocked)
MP| Wisdom
Melee Damage| Strength
Accuracy| Dexterity

---

🍀 Luck System (Summary)

Luck is:

- Rolled as 3d6
- Invoked by the player
- Resolved using:
  - Odd/Even guess
  - GM rolls 1d20

Effects:

- Success → Positive modifier + narrative effect
- Failure → Negative modifier + consequence

«Luck is not passive and must be declared»


🔹 GM Mercy Rule

If a character is unplayable:

- Reroll one stat
  OR
- Apply +2 to one stat

---

title: Characteristics — Tier System
content:
## Overview

All **Primary Characteristics** are numerical values that scale upward over time  
(e.g. Strength 47, Intelligence 82).

Every **20 points** in a characteristic grants a **Tier**.

- There are **5 Tiers**
- Each Tier provides a **flat bonus**
- Bonuses apply to:
  - The primary characteristic
  - All skills directly governed by that characteristic
- **Secondary Characteristics** only gain bonuses if unlocked via a relevant **Profession**

This system allows fine numerical growth while keeping bonuses clean, readable, and balanced.

## Characteristic Tier Bands

|   Tier   | Characteristic Value | Bonus |
| :------: | :------------------: | :---: |
|  Tier I  |         1–19         |  +0   |
| Tier II  |        20–39         |  +1   |
| Tier III |        40–59         |  +2   |
| Tier IV  |        60–79         |  +3   |
|  Tier V  |       80–100+        |  +4   |
> Values above 100 still count as **Tier V**, unless a special rule says otherwise.

>These bonuses are **always on** and do not require rolls to activate.

## How Tier Bonuses Apply

### Primary Characteristics

Tier bonus applies to:
- Any roll using the **raw characteristic**
- Any skill that lists that characteristic as its **Primary Attribute**

**Example:**  
Strength 54 → Tier III → **+2**
- +2 to Strength checks
- +2 to Athletics, Grappling, Heavy Weapons, etc.

### Skills (Primary Attribute)
If a skill lists a **Primary Characteristic**, it automatically benefits from the tier bonus.

| Skill | Primary Characteristic |
|-----|------------------------|
| Athletics | Strength |
| Archery | Dexterity |
| Blacksmithing | Strength |
| Alchemy | Intelligence |
| Administration | Intelligence |
| Survival | Wisdom |
### Secondary Characteristics (Profession Locked)

Secondary characteristics:
- Do **not** automatically gain tier bonuses
- Must be **unlocked via a relevant Profession**
- Once unlocked, they benefit from the **same tier bonus** as their parent characteristic

**Example:**  
- Endurance is a secondary characteristic under Constitution
- Endurance bonuses only apply if unlocked by a profession (Soldier, Ranger, etc.)

---

## Example Calculations
#### Example 1 — Strength

- Strength: **67**
- Tier: **IV (60–79)**
- Tier Bonus: **+3**

Applies to:
- Strength checks
- Melee damage scaling
- Athletics skill
- Blacksmithing skill

#### Example 2 — Intelligence & Alchemy

- Intelligence: **82**
- Tier: **V**
- Tier Bonus: **+4**

Applies to:
- Intelligence checks
- Alchemy
- Administration
- Lore-based skills

Does **not** automatically apply to:
- Mana pool (unless defined elsewhere)
- Secondary characteristics unless unlocked

title: Luck System
content:
# Luck System

Luck represents **fate, coincidence, and narrative probability**.  
It is not passive and must be **invoked** by the player.

Luck can result in **good fortune or misfortune**.

---

## Core Resolution Rule (LOCKED)

When a player invokes Luck:

1. The player declares **Odd** or **Even**
2. The **GM rolls 1d20 in secret**
3. If the guess is correct → 🍀 **Good Luck**
4. If incorrect → ☠ **Bad Luck**

Luck is resolved **before** the outcome of the action.

---

## Luck Tier Structure

Luck uses the **same Tier structure as all other Characteristics**.  
The Tier determines the **numeric modifier** and the **scope of narrative effect**.

### Luck Tiers & Modifiers

| Luck Tier    | LCK Range | Numeric Effect |
| ------------ | --------- | -------------- |
| **Tier I**   | 1–20      | ±0             |
| **Tier II**  | 21–40     | ±1             |
| **Tier III** | 41–60     | ±2             |
| **Tier IV**  | 61–80     | ±3             |
| **Tier V**   | 81–100    | ±4             |

- **Good Luck** → apply the **+ value**
- **Bad Luck** → apply the **− value**
- Modifier applies to the **current roll or outcome only**

Luck is never permanent.

---

## How Luck Is Applied

When Luck is invoked, it always has **two layers**:

1. **Fixed Numeric Effect** (automatic)
2. **Narrative Effect** (GM-approved or GM-offered)

---

## Numeric Luck Effects (Automatic)

The Luck modifier may be applied to:
- Emphasis rolls
- Death saving throws
- Skill checks marked **[Luck-Enabled]**
- Resistance checks
- Consequence severity rolls
- Critical confirmation rolls

The numeric modifier is **always applied**, even if the narrative effect is declined.

---

## Narrative Luck Effects (Optional but Encouraged)

Narrative effects are:
- Offered by the GM **or**
- Requested by the player
- Always subject to GM approval

Narrative effects scale by **Luck Tier**.

---

## Narrative Effects by Tier

### 🍀 Good Luck – Narrative Scope

| Tier | Narrative Impact |
|----|------------------|
| I | Minor convenience or coincidence |
| II | Helpful circumstance or reduced cost |
| III | Significant advantage or complication avoidance |
| IV | Major twist in the character’s favour |
| V | Exceptional or story-altering moment |

Examples:
- A door is unlocked
- A missed attack becomes a glancing blow
- Reinforcements arrive late
- A lethal fall becomes survivable
- A soul avoids permanent damage

---

### ☠ Bad Luck – Narrative Severity

| Tier | Narrative Severity |
|----|--------------------|
| I | Severe complication |
| II | Strong setback |
| III | Moderate consequence |
| IV | Minor complication |
| V | Reduced or twisted misfortune |

Examples:
- Equipment damage
- Social fallout
- Lingering injury
- Escalation of danger
- Unintended attention

High Luck does not prevent bad luck — it **softens it**.

---

## Special Applications

### Emphasis Rolls
- **Good Luck:** Apply +Tier modifier and one narrative benefit
- **Bad Luck:** Apply −Tier modifier and a complication

---

### Death Saving Throws
Luck may be invoked **once per death sequence**.

**Good Luck**
- Apply +Tier modifier
- Narrative options:
  - Convert a failure to a success
  - Stabilise at 1 HP
  - Avoid a lingering injury

**Bad Luck**
- Apply −Tier modifier
- Narrative options:
  - Failure counts as two failures
  - Lingering injury even on survival
  - Soul strain or scar

---

### Skills & Talents
- Only skills or talents marked **[Luck-Enabled]** may invoke Luck freely
- Other uses require GM approval
- Certain talents may:
  - Reduce Bad Luck severity
  - Expand narrative options
  - Allow Luck use without rolling (very rare)

---

## GM Safety Rules

- Luck should be used in **high-stakes moments**
- GM may limit Luck use in trivial scenes
- Narrative effects should always create **story momentum**
- Luck should never feel safe or routine

---

## Design Notes

- Chance is always **50/50**
- Luck Tier controls **impact, not probability**
- Numeric effects keep Luck mechanically relevant
- Narrative effects keep Luck dramatic
- System scales cleanly to **Level 100**

---

## Related Pages
- [[Luck Characteristic]]
- [[Emphasis Rolls]]
- [[Death Saving Throws]]
- [[Skill System]]
- [[Talent System]]

title: Characteristics System Core
content:
# 🧬 Characteristics System (Core)

## 📖 Overview
Characteristics represent the fundamental attributes of a character in Asteria.  
They influence:
- Skill checks
- Resource pools (HP, SP, MP)
- Combat effectiveness
- Passive bonuses via Tier scaling

Each Characteristic scales through a **Tier System**, granting increasing bonuses as values rise.

---

## 📊 Core Characteristics

| Characteristic | Description | Primary Function |
|---------------|------------|------------------|
| **Strength** | Raw physical power | Melee damage, lifting, force actions |
| **Dexterity** | Precision and control | Accuracy, fine manipulation |
| **Agility** | Speed and reaction | Initiative, evasion |
| **Constitution** | Physical durability | 🔴 HP Modifier |
| **Endurance** | Stamina and exertion | 🟢 SP Modifier |
| **Intelligence** | Knowledge and reasoning | Spell complexity, crafting |
| **Wisdom** | Awareness and control | 🔵 MP Modifier |
| **Charisma** | Presence and influence | Social interaction |
| **Luck** | Fate and randomness | Unpredictable outcomes |

---

## 🔗 Resource Links

| Resource | Linked Characteristic | Effect |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| 🔴 **HP (Health Points)** | Constitution | Increases maximum health |
| 🟢 **SP (Stamina Points)** | Endurance | Increases stamina pool |
| 🔵 **MP (Mana Points)** | Wisdom | Increases mana pool |

> ⚠️ No time-based scaling multipliers are applied. All increases are flat or system-defined.

---

## 📈 Characteristic Tiers

Each Characteristic scales in **20-point increments**, forming 5 tiers.

| Tier | Value Range | Bonus Effect |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Tier 1** | 1–20 | Base capability |
| **Tier 2** | 21–40 | Minor bonus |
| **Tier 3** | 41–60 | Moderate bonus |
| **Tier 4** | 61–80 | Major bonus |
| **Tier 5** | 81–100 | Peak capability |

### 🎯 Tier Effects
- Tier increases grant:
  - Improved skill effectiveness
  - Increased reliability in checks
  - Passive bonuses (defined per system)
- Skills linked to a Characteristic benefit from its Tier

---

## 🎲 Skill Check Interaction

### ⚔️ Combat & Combat-Related Skills
- Use: **D20 System**
- Includes:
  - Attacks
  - Dodging
  - Blocking
  - Movement in combat

### 🛠️ Non-Combat Skills
- Use: **D100 System**
- Includes:
  - Crafting
  - Gathering
  - Knowledge checks
  - Social interactions

---

## 🔗 Characteristic → Skill Links

### 💪 Strength
- Melee Weapons
- Heavy Lifting
- Athletics

### 🎯 Dexterity
- Ranged Weapons
- Precision Crafting
- Sleight of Hand

### ⚡ Agility
- Initiative
- Dodging
- Acrobatics

### ❤️ Constitution
- Fortitude
- Resistance Checks

### 🟢 Endurance
- Sprinting
- Survival (physical)
- Sustained actions

### 🧠 Intelligence
- Arcana
- Engineering
- Alchemy
- Enchanting

### 🔵 Wisdom
- Perception
- Mana Control
- Insight

### 👑 Charisma
- Persuasion
- Deception
- Leadership

### 🎲 Luck
- Critical Events
- Loot Rolls
- Random Outcomes

---

## 🎲 Luck System (Special Rule)

- All characters roll **3D6** for Luck at creation
- Luck can result in **positive OR negative effects**
- When Luck is triggered:
  1. Player chooses **Odd or Even**
  2. GM rolls a **D20**
  3. If correct → Positive outcome  
     If incorrect → Negative outcome

### ✨ Luck Influence
- Critical moments
- Death saves (Rule of Cool applicable)
- Rare events & loot
- Narrative twists

---

## 🧩 Notes for Players & GM

- Characteristics are increased using **CP (Characteristic Points)**
- Tier progression is one of the **most impactful scaling systems**
- Skills do NOT function in isolation—they are enhanced by their linked Characteristic
- Secondary effects (advanced scaling, profession bonuses) may modify how Characteristics apply

title: Encounter XP System
content:
## XP Calculation
Encounter XP =
Threat Tier Base XP × Size Modifier × Magical Modifier 
- + Creature Type Flat XP Bonus
- x Role Modifier (If Required)

## Threat Tier Table (Levels 1–100)

| Threat Tier | Level Range | Name            | Description             | Base XP    |
| ----------- | ----------- | --------------- | ----------------------- | ---------- |
| **TT 1**    | 1–5         | Trivial         | No real danger          | 25         |
| **TT 2**    | 6–10        | Minor           | Dangerous to novices    | 50         |
| **TT 3**    | 11–15       | Low             | Trained combatants      | 100        |
| **TT 4**    | 16–20       | Standard        | Adventurer-level threat | 200        |
| **TT 5**    | 21–25       | Dangerous       | Can down PCs            | 400        |
| **TT 6**    | 26–30       | Elite           | Tactical threat         | 800        |
| **TT 7**    | 31–35       | Champion        | Forces planning         | 1,600      |
| **TT 8**    | 36–40       | Mini-Boss       | Party-wide danger       | 3,200      |
| **TT 9**    | 41–45       | Boss            | Central encounter       | 6,400      |
| **TT 10**   | 46–50       | Major Boss      | Campaign-defining       | 12,800     |
| **TT 11**   | 51–55       | Mythic          | Beyond mortal           | 25,600     |
| **TT 12**   | 56–60       | Mythic Champion | Superhuman              | 51,200     |
| **TT 13**   | 61–65       | Demigod         | Alters regions          | 102,400    |
| **TT 14**   | 66–70       | Lesser Deity    | Alters nations          | 204,800    |
| **TT 15**   | 71–75       | Godlike         | Alters continents       | 409,600    |
| **TT 16**   | 76–80       | Avatar          | World-scale threat      | 819,200    |
| **TT 17**   | 81–85       | Primordial      | Reality pressure        | 1,638,400  |
| **TT 18**   | 86–90       | Cosmic          | Planar influence        | 3,276,800  |
| **TT 19**   | 91–95       | Existential     | Multiversal danger      | 6,553,600  |
| **TT 20**   | 96–100      | Absolute        | World-ending threat     | 13,107,200 |

## Size Modifier Table

| Size Category | XP Modifier |
| ------------- | ----------- |
| Miniature     | ×0.25       |
| Tiny          | ×0.50       |
| Small         | ×0.75       |
| Medium        | ×1.0        |
| Large         | ×1.25       |
| X-Large       | ×1.50       |
| Huge          | ×2.0        |
| Mammoth       | ×2.50       |
| Gargantuan    | ×3.0        |
| Colossal      | ×4.0        |
# Magical vs Non-Magical Modifier

This modifier represents whether a creature is **inherently supernatural**.

| Creature Type    | Description                               | XP Modifier |
| ---------------- | ----------------------------------------- | ----------- |
| Non-Magical      | Exists without supernatural influence     | ×1.0        |
| Minor Magical    | Sustained, empowered, or created by magic | ×1.30       |
| Superior magical |                                           | X1.75       |

---

#### Classification Rules

A creature is considered **Magical** if **any** of the following are true:
- Uses supernatural or spell-like abilities
- Has magical resistances or immunities
- Is undead, construct, elemental, fae, or extraplanar
- Could not exist in a non-magical world

Otherwise, the creature is **Non-Magical**.

# Creature Type Flat XP Bonus

This table grants a **flat XP bonus** based on creature type.  
It represents **complexity, special rules, and narrative weight**, not power.

- Applied **once per creature**
- Added **after all XP calculations**
- Does **not scale with level**

---

## Creature Type XP Bonus Table

| Creature Type | Includes                                            | Flat XP Bonus |
| ------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ------------- |
| **Animal**    | Natural animals, mounts                             | +10 XP        |
| **Plant**     | Living flora, fungal beings                         | +50 XP        |
| **Humanoid**  | All humanoid races (any size), demi-races, beastkin | +75 XP        |
| **Construct** | Golems, artificial beings                           | +75 XP        |
| **Undead**    | Zombies, spirits, liches                            | +75 XP        |
| **Beast**     |                                                     | +150 XP       |
| **Elemental** | Fire, water, earth, air beings                      | +             |
| **Spirit**    | Ethereal, incorporeal entities                      | +             |
| **Fae**       | Fae races, courts, tricksters                       | +500 XP       |
| **Fiend**     | Demonic and infernal beings                         | +500 XP       |
| **Celestial** | Angelic, divine beings                              | +500 XP       |
| **Monstrous** |                                                     | +1,000 XP     |
| **Dragon**    | True dragons, dragon entities                       | +1,500 XP     |
| **Gods**      | Avatars, lesser gods, divine manifestations         | +2,000 XP     |
# Role Modifier - Group Encounter

| Role     | Modifier |
| -------- | -------- |
| Minion   | x0.5     |
| Standard | x1.0     |
| Elite    | x1.5     |
| Boss     | x2.0     |

title: Soul Point Level System
content:
## Overview
**Soul Points** represent condensed essence taken from living or once-living beings.  
They are not experience, currency, or magic — they are **potential**, **identity**, and **power**.

Soul Points are dangerous to gather, valuable to trade, and transformative to use.

## Soul Level Progression

Soul Level represents how much soul energy a character can safely contain.

| Soul Level | Title (Optional) | Max Stored Soul Points |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| Rank 1 | Tainted | 25 |
| Rank 2 | Stirred | 75 |
| Rank 3 | Awakened | 150 |
| Rank 4 | Tempered | 300 |
| Rank 5 | Luminous | 600 |
| Rank 6 | Brilliant | 1,200 |
| Rank 7 | Ascendant | 2,500 |
| Rank 8 | Resplendent | 5,000 |

> Exceeding the maximum causes **Soul Strain** (see below).

---

## Gaining Soul Points

Soul Points are gained by:
- Slaying powerful creatures
- Extracting souls into **Soul Stones**
- Consuming or binding Soul Stones
- Certain forbidden rituals
- World or narrative events

Soul Points are **never gained passively**.

---

## Soul Stone Ranks (Existing System)

| Rank | Quality | Soul Points Stored |
|----|--------|--------------------|
| 1 | Poor | 1–5 |
| 2 | Weak | 5–15 |
| 3 | Basic | 15–25 |
| 4 | Common | 25–50 |
| 5 | Luminous | 50–100 |
| 6 | Brilliant | 100–200 |
| 7 | Special | 200–500 |
| 8 | Resplendent | 500–1,000 |

Soul Stones may be:
- Stored
- Traded
- Consumed
- Used in crafting
- Used as ritual components

---

## Using Soul Points

Soul Points may be spent to:

### Permanent Effects
- Increase **Soul Level**
- Unlock forbidden abilities
- Enhance Skill Infinity (GM approval)
- Bind legendary items
- Alter one’s nature (undead, ascendant, hybrid)

### Temporary Effects
- Boost d100 rolls
- Ignore fatal damage
- Empower spells or techniques
- Resist death or soul damage

> Soul Points spent are **gone forever**.

---

## Soul Level Advancement

Advancing a Soul Level requires:
1. Reaching the Soul Point threshold
2. A dangerous ritual, trial, or event
3. GM approval

| Next Soul Level | Soul Points Required (Spent) |
|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Rank 2 | 50 |
| Rank 3 | 150 |
| Rank 4 | 350 |
| Rank 5 | 750 |
| Rank 6 | 1,500 |
| Rank 7 | 3,000 |
| Rank 8 | 6,000 |

Advancement is **irreversible**.

---

## Soul Strain (Hard Limit)

If a character holds more Soul Points than their Soul Level allows:

- Gain **Soul Strain**
- Effects may include:
  - Hallucinations
  - Unstable magic
  - Soul corruption
  - Attracting entities
  - Loss of identity

Prolonged strain can lead to:
- Possession
- Mutation
- Soul fracture
- Permanent character change

---

## Soul Damage & Loss

Certain effects can:
- Drain Soul Points
- Shatter Soul Stones
- Reduce Soul Level temporarily
- Permanently scar the soul

Soul loss is far harder to recover than HP or XP.

---

## Death & Souls

On death:
- Stored Soul Points may:
  - Spill into the world
  - Be captured by enemies
  - Bind the character to locations, items, or beings
- Resurrection may:
  - Cost Soul Points
  - Reduce Soul Level
  - Alter the character

---

## Soul Economy & Morality

Soul usage is often:
- Illegal
- Taboo
- Feared
- Controlled by factions

Some cultures:
- Worship souls
- Trade them openly
- Hunt soul users
- Burn Soul Stones on sight

---

## Design Intent
- Soul Points are **rare and powerful**
- They create meaningful choices
- They are never required, but always tempting
- Power gained through souls has consequences

---

## Summary
- Soul Points are a separate progression system
- Soul Level limits storage and safety
- Soul Stones are vessels, not power
- Using souls changes the character permanently
- Great power always leaves a mark

# Soul System – Core Rules Expansion

## Universal Truth of Souls
**All living things possess a soul.**

This includes:
- Humanoids
- Beasts
- Monsters
- Magical creatures
- Plants (weak, diffuse souls)
- Constructs with living cores
- Summoned beings (temporary or bound souls)

Souls vary in **strength, clarity, and stability**, which determines their Soul Point value.

---

## Capturing a Soul

Souls **cannot be captured naturally**.  
A soul can only be bound into a **Soul Stone** through **magic**.

### Required Magic Schools
To capture a soul, the caster must use a spell from one of the following schools:

- **Life Magic**
- **Death Magic**

Other schools **cannot** capture souls unless explicitly stated.

---

## Soul Capture Conditions

A soul may only be captured if **all conditions are met**:

1. The creature is **dying or has just died**
2. A valid **Soul Stone** is present
3. A **Soul Binding Spell** is successfully cast
4. The soul is not protected, claimed, or destroyed

Failure may result in:
- Soul dissipation
- Stone cracking or shattering
- Soul backlash
- Spiritual corruption

---

## Life vs Death Magic (Important Distinction)

### Life Magic – Preservation Binding
- Captures the soul **intact**
- Soul Points remain at full value
- Lower corruption risk
- Preferred for:
  - Crafting
  - Enchanting
  - Resurrection rituals
  - Sanctified items

### Death Magic – Severance Binding
- Tears the soul free by force
- May reduce Soul Point yield
- Higher corruption risk
- Preferred for:
  - Dark enchanting
  - Necromancy
  - Curses
  - Soul weapons

> Life preserves.  
> Death takes.

---

## Soul Stones as Vessels

Soul Stones:
- Do **not generate power**
- Only **contain** soul energy
- Prevent immediate soul decay

A Soul Stone’s **rank** determines:
- Maximum Soul Points it can hold
- Stability of the captured soul
- Risk of leakage or corruption

(Refer to *Soul Stone Rank Table*.)

---

## Soul Point Usage

Soul Points are a **crafting resource**, not experience.

They may be spent on:

### Enchanting
- Fuel enchantment strength
- Extend enchantment duration
- Add additional effects
- Stabilise unstable magic

### Crafting Components
- Replace rare materials
- Enhance item quality tiers
- Bind abilities to items
- Create living or semi-living items

### Ritual Components
- Power large-scale rituals
- Anchor planar effects
- Bind entities
- Alter locations or environments

Soul Points spent are **consumed permanently**.

---

## Soul Purity & Quality

Not all Soul Points are equal.

Factors affecting quality:
- Creature intelligence
- Magical affinity
- Emotional intensity at death
- Willpower or resistance
- Method of capture (Life vs Death)

Low-quality souls may:
- Be unstable
- Corrupt items
- Degrade over time

High-quality souls may:
- Enhance crafting outcomes
- Reduce crafting difficulty
- Add unique traits

---

## Ethical & World Impact

Soul capture is:
- Often illegal
- Heavily regulated
- Feared or hated
- Closely watched by factions, gods, and spirits

Repeated soul use may:
- Attract soul-hunters
- Mark the character spiritually
- Alter NPC reactions
- Change how magic interacts with the character

---

## Failure & Consequences

Failed soul capture or misuse can cause:
- Soul Stone destruction
- Soul backlash damage
- Corruption conditions
- Hauntings or manifestations
- Permanent soul scars

Souls are not inert fuel — they **remember**.

---

title: Storyline XP System
content:
# XP Additions & Threat Factors (Flat Values)

All XP listed here is **added on top of Base Threat Tier XP**.  
These are **flat values**, not multipliers.

Apply only what meaningfully increases danger.

---

## 1. Intelligence & Tactics

| Intelligence Level | Description | XP |
|-------------------|-------------|----|
| Mindless | Pure instinct, no tactics | +0 XP |
| Cunning | Ambushes, flanking, terrain use | +150 XP |
| Tactical | Formations, retreats, focus fire | +350 XP |
| Strategic | Traps, contingencies, layered plans | +700 XP |

**Design Note:**  
Strategic enemies should feel noticeably more dangerous than stat-heavy foes.

---

## 4. Numbers & Formation (Group Threat)

| Group Type | Description | XP |
|-----------|-------------|----|
| Lone | Single creature | +0 XP |
| Pack | Loose coordination | +200 XP |
| Trained Unit | Military drills, formations | +450 XP |
| Swarm / Warband | Overwhelming numbers or phalanx | +800 XP |

**Design Note:**  
Apply once per encounter, not per creature.

---

## 5. Environment & Lair Advantage

| Environment | Description | XP |
|------------|-------------|----|
| Neutral Ground | No advantage | +0 XP |
| Familiar Territory | Knows terrain well | +200 XP |
| Lair | Traps, lair actions, choke points | +500 XP |
| Hostile Environment | Actively harms players | +900 XP |

**Examples:**
- Lava chambers  
- Shadow-corrupted forest  
- Collapsing ruins  

---

## 6. Morale & Fanaticism

| Morale Level | Description | XP |
|-------------|-------------|----|
| Cowardly | Flees early | −150 XP |
| Normal | Breaks under pressure | +0 XP |
| Fanatical | Fights to near-death | +400 XP |
| Death-Bound | Cannot flee or surrender | +800 XP |

**Design Note:**  
Death-bound enemies dramatically increase lethality — XP reflects that.

---

## 7. Narrative Importance

| Narrative Role | Description | XP |
|---------------|-------------|----|
| Random | No story weight | +0 XP |
| Named Enemy | Recognised individual | +250 XP |
| Faction Leader | Commands others | +600 XP |
| Campaign Figure | Major plot anchor | +1,200 XP |

**Design Note:**  
This ensures bosses and story figures always feel rewarding.

---

## 9. Corruption / Influence Radius

| Influence Level | Description | XP |
|----------------|-------------|----|
| None | No lasting impact | +0 XP |
| Local | Village or site affected | +300 XP |
| Regional | Entire region altered | +700 XP |
| World-Touching | Reality-altering presence | +1,500 XP |

**Examples:**
- Blighted land  
- Undead spread  
- Madness aura  

---

## 10. Victory Conditions (How the Encounter Was Won)

| Outcome | Description | XP |
|-------|-------------|----|
| Routed | Enemy collapsed quickly | −200 XP |
| Standard Victory | Expected challenge | +0 XP |
| Hard-Fought | Resource-draining win | +600 XP |
| Clutch Survival | Near party wipe | +1,200 XP |

**Design Note:**  
Rewards risk and tension, not speed.

---

## 11. Time Pressure

| Pressure Level | Description | XP |
|---------------|-------------|----|
| None | No urgency | +0 XP |
| Timed | Countdown present | +250 XP |
| Escalating | Threat worsens over time | +600 XP |
| Catastrophic | Failure causes major loss | +1,200 XP |

**Examples:**
- Ritual nearing completion  
- Flooding dungeon  
- Collapsing battlefield  

---

## 12. Unknown or Hidden Threats

| Knowledge Level | Description | XP |
|----------------|-------------|----|
| Known | Fully understood enemy | +0 XP |
| Partial Intel | Some unknown abilities | +300 XP |
| Hidden Threat | Surprise phase or form | +700 XP |
| Revelation | Major twist mid-encounter | +1,300 XP |

**Examples:**
- Second phase  
- Hidden transformation  
- Sudden reinforcement wave  

---

## XP Design Rule

> XP rewards **danger, pressure, and consequence**, not combat length.

If it:
- Forces new tactics  
- Raises emotional stakes  
- Introduces uncertainty  

…it earns XP.

---

title: 🔒 Level & Tier Structure (Core System)
content:
## Overview
- **Character Levels:** 0–100  
- **Tier System:** 5 fixed tiers  
- **Tier Size:** 20 levels per tier  
- **Progression Type:** Linear XP per tier  
- **Status:** LOCKED (Foundational System)

Levels represent overall experience, power, and narrative stature.  
Tiers define scope, reputation, and the scale at which a character operates.

---

## Level 0 — Unproven State
**XP Required:** 0  

Level 0 represents:
- Civilians
- Apprentices
- Newly awakened characters
- Pre-adventuring backgrounds

Rules:
- No tier bonuses apply
- No tier XP rewards apply
- Level 1 is the first true adventuring level

---

## Tier Structure (LOCKED)

| Tier     | Levels | Narrative Identity | Tier Dice Modifier |
| -------- | ------ | ------------------ | ------------------ |
| Tier I   | 1–20   | Mortal / Initiate  | +0                 |
| Tier II  | 21–40  | Veteran            | +1                 |
| Tier III | 41–60  | Heroic             | +2                 |
| Tier IV  | 61–80  | Mythic             | +4                 |
| Tier V   | 81–100 | Legendary          | +6                 |

Rules:
- Tier boundaries are permanent
- No system may subdivide tiers
- Tier size may never change

---

## XP Progression by Tier (LOCKED)

XP required to advance **each level within a tier**:

| Tier | Levels | XP per Level |
|------|--------|--------------|
| Tier I | 1–20 | +10,000 XP |
| Tier II | 21–40 | +20,000 XP |
| Tier III | 41–60 | +30,000 XP |
| Tier IV | 61–80 | +40,000 XP |
| Tier V | 81–100 | +50,000 XP |

Rules:
- XP progression is linear within a tier
- XP increases only at tier boundaries
- No percentage-based scaling is used

---

## Tier XP Milestone Rewards (LOCKED)

Granted **once**, immediately upon entering a new tier.

| Tier Entered | Level | XP Reward |
|--------------|-------|-----------|
| Tier II | 21 | +5,000 XP |
| Tier III | 41 | +10,000 XP |
| Tier IV | 61 | +15,000 XP |
| Tier V | 81 | +20,000 XP |

Rules:
- Tier XP rewards can never cause an automatic level-up
- Tier XP rewards are flat values
- Tier XP rewards are non-repeatable

---

## Design Intent (Canonical)

- **Levels** represent overall growth and experience  
- **Tiers** represent scope, reputation, and narrative scale  
- **Skills** represent mastery  
- **Talents** represent specialization  

Tiers define what a character *can be*, not how fast they advance.

---

title: Level-Up Rewards
content:
When a character gains a level, they receive rewards that represent **growth in skill, power, and identity**.

Higher levels grant increasingly impactful rewards.

#### Core Level-Up Rewards (Every Level)
Each time a character levels up, they gain:
- A new Level
- **+3 Characteristic Points**
- **+6 Talent Points**

- Milestone Talent Points

##### Every 5 Levels ????
At levels **5, 10, 15, 20, etc.**, the character gains:
- +1 Skill Proficiency **or** Improve an existing proficiency???
- **+1 Secondary Characteristic**  
  *(Player choice or GM-approved)*

> These can be spent immediately or saved.

## GM Guidance

> [!tip]
> Levels should change **how the character plays**, not just numbers.

title: XP System
content:
## What Is XP?
**Experience Points (XP)** represent a character’s **growth through action, risk, and consequence**.

XP is gained by:
- Overcoming dangerous encounters
- Surviving meaningful challenges
- Solving problems through combat, strategy, or roleplay
- Interacting with the world in impactful ways

XP does **not** represent time alone — it reflects **learning through adversity**.

---

## What XP Represents

XP is a measure of:
- **Combat experience** – fighting creatures, monsters, and enemies
- **Adaptation** – learning how to survive new threats
- **Knowledge** – understanding magic, creatures, cultures, and tactics
- **Legend** – the growing reputation and myth of the character

As XP increases, characters are no longer just skilled — they become **forces that shape the world**.

---

## How XP Is Awarded
#### Combat Based XP
XP is awarded for:
- **Defeating or overcoming enemies** (not just killing them)
- **Completing encounters**, including social or exploration challenges
- **Clever solutions** that avoid unnecessary conflict
- **Surviving encounters** that could reasonably have killed the party

#### Story Based XP
XP may be awarded even if:
- The enemy escapes
- The challenge is resolved diplomatically
- The objective is achieved without combat
> 
#### Skill Based XP
Skill XP is granted when a character:
- Successfully uses a skill in a **meaningful situation**
- Succeeds at a **high-risk or high-DC skill check**
- Uses a skill creatively to **solve a problem**
- Practices a skill under **pressure or consequence**
- Advances a skill’s **rank**

>Skill XP is **not** awarded for trivial or repeated actions with no risk.

title: 🔒 Skill Systems – Core Framework
content:
## Skill Categories
Skills fall into three application categories:
- **Combat Skills** (weapons, defense, tactics)
- **Crafting Skills** (smithing, alchemy, enchanting, professions)
- **Utility / Non-Combat Skills** (stealth, persuasion, survival, knowledge)

All skills share the **same progression rules**, but apply XP differently based on category.

## Skill Rank, Modifiers Structure & XP Requirements
There are **7 permanent skill ranks**.

| Skill Rank  | Modifier | XP Required |
| ----------- | -------- | ----------- |
| Novice      | –2       | 500         |
| Initiate    | –1       | 1,500       |
| Apprentice  | 0        | 3,000       |
| Journeyman  | +1       | 6,000       |
| Adept       | +3       | 12,000      |
| Master      | +5       | 24,000      |
| Grandmaster | +7       | 48,000      |
#### Affinity Roll System
Skills use a **D100 Infinity Roll** to represent mastery ceilings.

| Skill Rank | Infinity Range |
|-----------|----------------|
| Novice | 01–09 |
| Initiate | 10–24 |
| Apprentice | 25–44 |
| Journeyman | 45–69 |
| Adept | 70–84 |
| Master | 85–97 |
| Grandmaster | 98–100 |
#### Skill Rank → Character XP Rewards
One-time **character XP rewards** when a skill reaches a new rank.

| Skill Rank Achieved | Character XP |
|--------------------|--------------|
| Novice | +100 XP |
| Initiate | +250 XP |
| Apprentice | +500 XP |
| Journeyman | +1,000 XP |
| Adept | +2,000 XP |
| Master | +4,000 XP |
| Grandmaster | +8,000 XP |


## Skill Development 

**Granted:** Every 5 character levels.

### Choose ONE:
- **Unlock a new skill at Novice**
- **Gain +250 Skill XP** (assigned to one existing skill)

### Rules:
- No character XP granted
- No Affinity increase
- No immediate modifier change
- Skill XP must be assigned immediately
- Skill XP cannot be split or stored
- Skill XP cannot bypass tier-based rank caps

> "Any attempt to bypass these rules invalidates the choice."
---

## Skill XP From Actions – Core Rules

#### Universal Rules
- Skill XP is gained only from meaningful actions
- No XP without risk, cost, or consequence
- No XP for repetition or spam
- XP granted once per scene or task
- Maximum Skill XP per session per skill: **1,000 XP**
- Skill XP never grants ranks automatically

#### Combat Skill XP Application

| Trigger                                   | Skill XP   |
| ----------------------------------------- | ---------- |
| Meaningful hit or defense                 | 10–25 XP   |
| Tactical success (disarm, flank, control) | 25–50 XP   |
| Winning a dangerous encounter             | 50–100 XP  |
| Boss or elite combat                      | 100–150 XP |
#### Crafting Skill XP Application

| Craft Outcome | Skill XP |
|-------------|----------|
| Simple / routine item | 10–25 XP |
| Standard item | 25–50 XP |
| Complex item | 50–100 XP |
| Rare / dangerous craft | 100–200 XP |
| Masterwork / enchanted item | 150–300 XP |
#### Utility / Non-Combat Skill XP Application

| Situation | Skill XP |
|---------|----------|
| Minor meaningful success | 10–25 XP |
| High-risk success | 25–50 XP |
| Scene-defining use | 50–100 XP |
| Story-changing use | 100–200 XP |

## Tier Interaction Summary

| System | Tier Effect |
|----|------------|
| Level XP | Tier defines XP per level |
| Tier Milestone XP | Granted on tier entry |
| Skill XP Gain | Reduced by tier scaling |
| Skill Rank Caps | Tier-gated |
| Skill → Level XP | Scales safely with tiers |

title: Skill Affinity System
content:
## Overview
**Skill Affinity** represents a character’s *innate potential* in a specific skill — the absolute ceiling of mastery they can ever achieve.

While **Skill XP** determines *how fast* a character improves, **Skill Affinity** determines *how far* that improvement can go.

No amount of training, repetition, or XP can push a skill beyond its Affinity limit.

## What Skill Affinity Represents
Skill Infinity reflects:
- Natural aptitude
- Mental or physical limits
- Compatibility with the skill
- Talent, instinct, and intuition

Skill Affinity **does not represent**:
- Experience
- Practice
- Education
- Raw effort

Two characters may train the same skill for years and still reach different peaks due to differing Affinity values.

## Determining Skill Infinity
When a character **first learns a skill**, determine its Affinity value:

By rolling a **D100**

The roll may be made with:
- **Advantage** (roll twice, take the higher)
- **Disadvantage** (roll twice, take the lower)

Apply Advantage or Disadvantage only if justified by:
- Race or lineage  
- Background or upbringing  
- Class or profession  
- Magical influence  
- Curses or conditions  

## Skill Affinity Bands & Rank Caps

| Skill Rank Cap | Affinity Range | Rank Modifier | XP Required | Description         |
| -------------- | -------------- | ------------- | ----------- | ------------------- |
| Novice         | 01–09          | -2            | 500 XP      | Barely capable      |
| Initiate       | 10–24          | -1            | 1,500 XP    | Limited aptitude    |
| Apprentice     | 25–44          | 0             | 3,000 XP    | Average potential   |
| Journeyman     | 45–69          | +1            | 6,000 XP    | Strong aptitude     |
| Adept          | 70–84          | +2            | 12,000 XP   | Exceptional talent  |
| Master         | 85–97          | +3            | 24,000 XP   | Rare mastery        |
| Grandmaster    | 98–100         | +4            | 48,000 XP   | Legendary potential |

### Examples
- Affinity **33** → Maximum Rank: **Apprentice**  
- Affinity **57** → Maximum Rank: **Journeyman**  
- Affinity **71** → Maximum Rank: **Adept**  
- Affinity **100** → Maximum Rank: **Grandmaster**

---
## Interaction with Skill XP
- Skills gain XP through use, success, failure, training, and narrative events
- When XP reaches the threshold for the next rank:
  - The rank increases **only if allowed by Skill Affinity**
  - If Skill Infinity blocks progression, the rank does not increase

XP is **never lost** due to an Infinity cap.

---

## Reaching the Affinity Cap
Once a skill reaches its Affinity-determined maximum rank:
- The skill **cannot gain further ranks**
- The skill **can still be used normally**
- The skill **continues to gain XP**

Excess XP is converted into **Mastery Effects**.

---

## Mastery Effects (Post-Cap Progression)
Mastery Effects represent refinement rather than raw power.

Examples:
- Reduced chance of catastrophic failure  
- Increased consistency of results  
- Faster execution of routine tasks  
- Minor narrative advantages  
- Style, flair, or reputation benefits  

Mastery Effects **never increase modifiers** or bypass difficulty limits.

---

## No Guaranteed Success Rule
Skill Infinity **never grants certainty**.

All skill checks:
- Use a **d100 roll**
- May have Advantage or Disadvantage
- Always retain a chance of failure

Even a Grandmaster can fail under extreme conditions.

---

## Changing Skill Affinity
Skill Affinity is intentionally difficult to alter.

It may only be changed by:
- Divine intervention  
- Soul alteration  
- Realm ascension  
- Mythic artifacts  
- World-shaping narrative events  

Such changes are:
- Permanent  
- Rare  
- GM-controlled  
- Never achieved through XP alone  

---

title: Skill XP System
content:
# Crafting Complexity Tiers

Crafting complexity represents the **technical, conceptual, and material difficulty**
of an item, invention, or creation.

Crafting XP is awarded **once per completed project**, not per roll.

---

## Crafting Schematic Tier Table

| Schematic Tier | Description                                 | Base Crafting XP |
| -------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------- |
| Simple         | Basic, common items with known methods      | 0 XP             |
| Advanced       | Reliable gear requiring skill and precision | 250 XP           |
| Innovated      | New variations or improved designs          | 500 XP           |
| Ingenious      | Highly clever or multi-functional designs   | 1,000 XP         |
| Cutting Edge   | Pushing current technological limits        | 2,000 XP         |
| Revolutionary  | Changes how crafting is understood          | 3,000 XP         |
| Masterwork     | Near-perfect execution and design           | 4,000 XP         |
| Savant         | Legendary creations beyond mortal norms     | 5,000 XP         |

---

## Complexity Tier Descriptions

### Simple
- Ammunition, tools, rations
- No innovation or rare materials
- Often repeatable (XP only once)

### Advanced
- Weapons, armor, complex tools
- Requires trained craftsmanship
- Common among professionals

### Superior
- Enhanced gear, refined balance
- Requires high-quality materials
- Rare among common artisans

### Innovated
- New techniques or improvements
- Modifies existing designs
- Often requires experimentation

### Groundbreaking
- Never-before-seen designs
- Creates new crafting standards
- May attract attention or imitation

### Ingenious
- Multiple functions or layered mechanics
- Clever problem-solving baked into design
- Hard to replicate

### Cutting Edge
- At the edge of known capability
- Risk of failure is high
- Requires rare knowledge or tools

### State of the Art
- Best possible work in the current age
- Requires mastery and precision
- Sets the benchmark for others

### Revolutionary
- Alters industries, warfare, or magic
- Enables entirely new possibilities
- Often feared or controlled

### Masterwork
- Nearly flawless in form and function
- No wasted effort or material
- Extremely rare

### Serviant
- Legendary creations tied to myth, fate, or legacy
- Beyond standard mortal craftsmanship
- Often unique or world-defining

---

## Skill Rank Interaction

When a crafting project is completed, add the creator’s **Skill Rank Bonus**:

| Skill Rank | XP Bonus |
|-----------|----------|
| Novice | −50 XP |
| Initiate | +0 XP |
| Apprentice | +150 XP |
| Journeyman | +400 XP |
| Adept | +800 XP |
| Master | +1,600 XP |
| Grandmaster | +3,000 XP |

**Final Crafting XP = Base Crafting XP + Skill Rank Bonus**

---

## Failure & Risk XP

| Outcome | XP Awarded |
|-------|------------|
| Success | 100% Base XP |
| Partial Success | 50% Base XP |
| Major Failure | 25% Base XP |
| Catastrophic Failure | 0 XP + consequence |

---

## Anti-Farming Rules

- Repeating the **same design** grants no XP
- Mass production grants **no XP**
- XP only applies when:
  - New techniques are used
  - Stakes or cost are meaningful
  - Outcome affects the world

---

## Design Rule

> Crafting XP rewards **creation, innovation, and mastery** — not repetition.

Legendary items should feel earned **long before they feel powerful**.

---

### ⚙️ Calculation Rules
- **Total AC Modifier** = Base Material AC + Item Class Bonus + Item Quality Bonus  
- **Total Damage Modifier** = Base Material Dice + Item Class Damage + Item Quality Damage  
- **Example:**

---

### 🧭 Balancing Notes
- The **AC bonus** scales slightly faster to reflect defensive value in crafting.  
- The **damage bonus** is flatter — offensive scaling should come primarily from material and enchantment, not rarity stacking.  
- **Mythic and above** should start to feel legendary — ideal for world-tier or godforged weapons.
